examples.input: n=3 left nodes, m=3 right nodes, edges: L0-R0, L0-R1, L1-R1, L2-R2
examples.explanation: Start with empty matching {}. Process L0: try R0 (available) -> matching{R0:0}, recurse to L1. L1: try R1 (available) -> matching{R0:0, R1:1}, recurse to L2. L2: try R2 (available) -> matching{R0:0, R1:1, R2:2}, i=3 -> update max=3, backtrack. L2: no more neighbors, backtrack to L1. L1: try next neighbor? none, backtrack to L0. L0: try R1 (available) -> matching{R1:0}, recurse L1. L1: try R1 (occupied by 0, can we reassign? backtrack from L0? no because L0 has no other neighbor if we reassign, so skip), try other neighbors? none, backtrack to L0. L0: no more neighbors, done. Best found: size 3.

examples.input: n=2 left nodes, m=2 right nodes, edges: L0-R0, L1-R0
examples.explanation: Start empty {}. L0: try R0 -> {R0:0}, recurse L1. L1: try R0 (occupied by 0, can we reassign? backtrack from L0: L0 has no other neighbor, so cannot reassign) -> skip, no other neighbors -> backtrack to L0. L0: no more neighbors -> done. Best size=1. Alternative path: L0: skip? but algorithm tries all neighbors, so only R0. So maximum is 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking
approaches.summary: Try all possible matchings by recursively assigning each left node to any available right node, backtracking when conflicts arise. Use this only for very small graphs to understand the problem, as it's extremely inefficient.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force backtracking to find maximum matching in bipartite graphs.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force backtracking solution for maximum bipartite matching. The intuition is to systematically explore all possible matchings between left and right nodes by recursively assigning each left node to any compatible right node, then backtracking when we hit dead ends. We maintain a matching dictionary that maps right nodes to left nodes, and for each left node, we try all available right nodes (those not already matched or those we can reassign). The algorithm works by depth-first search through the space of all possible matchings. Time complexity is O(n! * m) because for n left nodes, we have n! possible matchings to explore, and each requires checking up to m edges. Space complexity is O(n + m) for storing the graph and recursion stack. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) by orchestrating the recursive exploration. Common mistakes include forgetting to backtrack properly (must undo assignments when returning from recursion) and not handling the case where a right node is already matched (must check if we can reassign it). Real code pattern: def backtrack(i, matching): if i == n: update max; return; for each neighbor j of left[i]: if j not in matching or backtrack(matching[j]): save old = matching.get(j); matching[j] = i; backtrack(i+1, matching); matching[j] = old if existed else delete.

examples.input: n=3 left nodes, m=3 right nodes, edges: L0-R0, L0-R1, L1-R1, L2-R2
examples.trace:
Start with empty matching {}. Process L0: try R0 (available) -> matching{R0:0}, recurse to L1. L1: try R1 (available) -> matching{R0:0, R1:1}, recurse to L2. L2: try R2 (available) -> matching{R0:0, R1:1, R2:2}, i=3 -> update max=3, backtrack. L2: no more neighbors, backtrack to L1. L1: try next neighbor? none, backtrack to L0. L0: try R1 (available) -> matching{R1:0}, recurse L1. L1: try R1 (occupied by 0, can we reassign? backtrack from L0? no because L0 has no other neighbor if we reassign, so skip), try other neighbors? none, backtrack to L
... (simplified)

examples.input: n=2 left nodes, m=2 right nodes, edges: L0-R0, L1-R0
examples.trace: Start empty {}. L0: try R0 -> {R0:0}, recurse L1. L1: try R0 (occupied by 0, can we reassign? backtrack from L0: L0 has no other neighbor, so cannot reassign) -> skip, no other neighbors -> backtrack to L0. L0: no more neighbors -> done. Best size=1. Alternative path: L0: skip? but algorithm tries all neighbors, so only R0. So maximum is 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Backtracking / DFS for Augmenting Path
ladders.desc: The recursive function that tries to match a specific left node to all possible right nodes.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function that, given a current left node index i and a current matching state, tries to assign this left node to each compatible right node, recurses to the next left node, and backtracks. The intuition is that we treat the matching as a state that we modify, explore, and restore. For left node i, we iterate through all its neighbor right nodes j. For each j, if j is not in the matching (unmatched), we assign matching[j]=i and recurse to i+1. If j is already matched to some other left node k, we attempt to reassign by recursively checking if k can be matched to a different right node (this is the backtracking step). The key insight is that by recursively exploring reassignments, we can potentially free up j for i. Time complexity for this subroutine in isolation is O(m) per call (checking all neighbors), but it triggers recursion. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly to build the complete solution. Common mistakes: not properly restoring the matching state after recursion (must save old value and put it back) and infinite recursion if reassignment check isn't bounded. Real code pattern: def try_match(i, matching): for j in adj[i]: if j not in matching: matching[j]=i; recurse(i+1); del matching[j]; else: old = matching[j]; matching[j]=i; if try_match(old, matching): recurse(i+1); matching[j]=old.

examples.input: Current left i=0, matching={}, adj[0]=[R0,R1]
examples.output: Explores assignments to R0 and R1
examples.trace: For i=0: iterate neighbors [R0,R1]. First j=R0: not in matching -> matching becomes {R0:0}, recurse to i=1. After recursion returns, restore: delete R0 from matching. Next j=R1: not in matching -> matching becomes {R1:0}, recurse to i=1. Restore: delete R1. Done.

examples.input: Current left i=1, matching={R0:0}, adj[1]=[R0]
examples.output: Attempts to reassign R0 from L0 to L1
examples.trace: For i=1: neighbor j=R0 is in matching (matched to 0). Save old=0. Set matching[R0]=1. Now recursively check if old left 0 can be rematched: call try_match(0, {R0:1}). adj[0] has R0 but it's taken, no other neighbors -> returns False. So reassignment fails. Restore matching[R0]=0. No other neighbors, so done.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Restoration / Augmenting Path Flipping
ladders.desc: The technique of modifying a state, recursing, and then restoring the state to explore alternatives.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the backtracking paradigm: we have a state (the current matching), we make a change (assign a left node to a right node), recursively explore further possibilities, and then undo the change to try other options. The intuition is that by saving and restoring state, we can explore all configurations without copying the entire state each time, saving memory. The algorithm: 1. Make a choice (e.g., assign left i to right j). 2. Recurse to explore subsequent choices (e.g., match remaining left nodes). 3. Undo the choice (restore matching to previous state). 4. Try next alternative. This ensures we exhaust all possibilities. Time complexity depends on number of states explored; here it's O(n!) in worst case. Space is O(n) for recursion stack plus O(n) for matching storage. This feeds into L1 as the mechanism used in the recursive function. Common mistakes: forgetting to undo changes (leads to incorrect state accumulation) and not handling base cases (when all left nodes processed). Real code pattern: state = {}; def backtrack(step): if step == n: process; return; for option in options: apply option to state; backtrack(step+1); revert option in state.

examples.input: State={}, options for step 0: assign A or B
examples.trace: Start state={}. Step 0: first option A -> state={A}, recurse step 1. After recursion returns, revert: state becomes {}. Next option B -> state={B}, recurse step 1. Revert: state={}. Done.

examples.input: State={X:1}, options for step 1: assign Y or Z
examples.trace: State={X:1}. Step 1: option Y -> state={X:1,Y:2}, recurse step 2. Return, revert Y: state={X:1}. Option Z -> state={X:1,Z:2}, recurse step 2. Return, revert Z: state={X:1}. Done.

testCases.input: state={}, steps=2, options per step: step0=['a'], step1=['b']
state={}, steps=1, options=['a'], with recursion depth 1

ladders.title: Building Block — Bipartite Graph Representation
ladders.desc: How to store a bipartite graph and iterate through neighbors of a node.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing a bipartite graph (with left and right partitions) and traversing it to access neighbors. Typically, we use an adjacency list: an array or list where index i corresponds to left node i, and the value is a list of right nodes that left i is connected to. The intuition is that this allows efficient access to all edges from a given left node, which is crucial for the brute force approach where we try all possible matches. The algorithm: 1. Initialize adj = [list() for _ in range(n)] where n is number of left nodes. 2. For each edge (u,v) where u is left node, v is right node, append v to adj[u]. 3. To traverse neighbors of left node i, iterate for j in adj[i]. Time complexity to build is O(E) where E is number of edges; traversal per node is O(degree(i)). Space is O(n + E). This feeds into L2 by providing the 'options' for each step in backtracking. Common mistakes: confusing left and right indices (ensure consistent numbering) and not handling duplicate edges (may need to deduplicate if graph has multiples). Real code pattern: n_left = 3; adj = [[] for _ in range(n_left)]; for edge in edges: u,v = edge; adj[u].append(v).

examples.input: Left nodes: 0,1; Right nodes: 0,1; Edges: (0,0), (0,1), (1,1)
examples.trace: Initialize adj for 2 left nodes: [[], []]. Process edge (0,0): append 0 to adj[0] -> [[0], []]. Edge (0,1): append 1 to adj[0] -> [[0,1], []]. Edge (1,1): append 1 to adj[1] -> [[0,1], [1]]. Done.

examples.input: Left nodes: 0; Right nodes: 0,1; Edges: (0,0), (0,0) duplicate
examples.trace: Initialize adj for 1 left: [[]]. First edge (0,0): append 0 -> [[0]]. Second edge (0,0): append 0 -> [[0,0]]. If deduplicating, might keep only unique, but algorithm may handle duplicates by trying same right node multiple times (inefficient but correct).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Matching Dictionary Updates
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations on a dictionary that maps right nodes to left nodes.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a dictionary (hash map) to represent a matching: keys are right nodes, values are the left node they are currently matched to. The intuition is that this allows O(1) average-time checks for whether a right node is matched and to which left node, and O(1) updates to assign or unassign. Key operations: 1. Check if right node j is matched: j in matching. 2. Get the left node matched to j: matching[j]. 3. Assign left i to right j: matching[j] = i. 4. Unassign right j: del matching[j] or matching.pop(j). 5. Save previous value before reassigning: old = matching.get(j). Time complexity for each is O(1) on average. Space is O(k) where k is number of matched pairs. This feeds into L3 as the state representation used in graph traversal. Common mistakes: using a list instead of dictionary for sparse right nodes (wastes space), not handling KeyError when accessing non-existent keys (use .get() or 'in' check), and not properly deleting entries when backtracking. Real code pattern: matching = {}; if j not in matching: matching[j] = i; else: old = matching[j]; matching[j] = i; ... later: matching[j] = old or del matching[j].

examples.input: matching={0:0}, check j=0, assign i=1 to j=0, then unassign
examples.trace: Start matching={0:0}. Check j=0: 0 in matching -> True, value is 0. Assign i=1: save old=0, set matching[0]=1 -> matching becomes {0:1}. Unassign: set matching[0]=old (0) -> matching back to {0:0}. Alternatively, if unassigning completely: del matching[0] -> {}.

examples.input: matching={}, check j=1, assign i=2 to j=1
examples.trace: Start matching={}. Check j=1: 1 not in matching -> False. Assign i=2: matching[1]=2 -> matching becomes {1:2}. Unassign: del matching[1] -> {}.

testCases.input: matching={}, j=0, i=0, then j=1, i=1
matching={0:0}, j=0, i=1, then revert to old

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Bipartite Matching Basics
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of bipartite graphs and maximum matching.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph has two sets of vertices with edges only between sets. Maximum matching pairs as many left vertices to right vertices as possible without sharing vertices. DFS finds augmenting paths to increase matching size iteratively.

examples.input: Left: {A,B}, Right: {1,2}, Edges: A-1, A-2, B-1
examples.output: Possible matchings: {A-1} or {A-2} or {B-1} or {A-1,B-1} invalid (shares 1)
examples.trace: Graph is bipartite: all edges go between left and right. Matchings: single edges like {A-1} (size 1), {A-2} (size 1), {B-1} (size 1). Cannot have {A-1,B-1} because both edges share right vertex 1, so not a valid matching. Maximum is size 1 (e.g., A-2 and B-1 cannot both be in matching because B-1 shares 1 with A-1 but A-2 is okay? Actually A-2 and B-1 is valid: edges A-2 and B-1 do not share vertices, so size 2 is possible! Let's check: A-2 uses A and 2, B-1 uses B and 1, no shared vertices, so maximum is 2. My earlier mistake: corrected.

examples.input: Left: {X}, Right: {Y,Z}, Edges: X-Y
examples.trace: Bipartite graph with one left node. Only edge is X-Y, so matching can only include that edge, size 1. X-Z not an edge, so cannot match.

approaches.name: DFS Augmenting Path
approaches.summary: Use depth-first search to find augmenting paths in the bipartite graph, flipping matches to increase the matching size iteratively. This is a standard method for bipartite matching, suitable for moderate-sized graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — DFS Augmenting Path
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of bipartite matching using DFS to find augmenting paths.
ladders.explanation: The bipartite matching problem involves finding the maximum number of edges in a bipartite graph such that no two edges share a vertex. The DFS augmenting path approach works by iteratively trying to match vertices from the left set to the right set. For each unmatched left vertex, we perform a DFS to find an augmenting path—a path that starts and ends at unmatched vertices, alternating between unmatched and matched edges. When found, we flip the matching status along this path, increasing the matching size by one. The intuition is that each augmenting path allows us to 'rearrange' existing matches to accommodate one more match. The algorithm runs in O(n * m) time where n is the number of vertices and m is the number of edges, as we perform DFS for each left vertex, each taking O(m) time in worst case. Space is O(n + m) for adjacency lists and visited arrays. This connects to L1 by using the DFS subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include not resetting visited arrays between DFS calls (leading to incorrect results) or handling graph representation incorrectly. Real code pattern: Initialize match arrays, iterate through left vertices, run DFS to find augmenting path, flip matches if found.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize matchR = [-1,-1,-1], visited = [False,False,False]. Step 2: For left vertex 0, DFS finds path 0->0 (unmatched), match 0 to 0, matchR[0]=0. Step 3: For left vertex 1, DFS finds path 1->1 (unmatched), match 1 to 1, matchR[1]=1. Step 4: For left vertex 2, DFS finds path 2->0 (matched to 0), try to rematch 0 via DFS from 0: 0->1 (unmatched), so path 2->0->1, flip matches: match 2 to 0 (matchR[0]=2), match 0 to 1 (matchR[1]=0). Now matching: 0-1, 1-?, 2-0. Step 5: Re-run for left vertex 1 (now unmatched), DFS finds path 1->1 (matc
... (simplified)

examples.input: Left vertices: [0,1], Right vertices: [0,1,2], Edges: [(0,0), (0,1), (1,1)]
examples.trace: Step 1: matchR = [-1,-1,-1], visited = [False,False,False]. Step 2: For left vertex 0, DFS finds path 0->0 (unmatched), match 0 to 0, matchR[0]=0. Step 3: For left vertex 1, DFS finds path 1->1 (unmatched), match 1 to 1, matchR[1]=1. No more left vertices, matching size 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Backtracking / DFS for Augmenting Path
ladders.desc: DFS function that finds an augmenting path for a given left vertex.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs a depth-first search from a left vertex to find an augmenting path in the bipartite graph. The goal is to find a path that ends at an unmatched right vertex, alternating between unmatched and matched edges. The intuition is that by exploring recursively, we can 'chain' through existing matches to free up a right vertex for the current left vertex. Steps: 1. Mark the current left vertex as visited. 2. For each neighbor (right vertex) of the left vertex: if the right vertex is unmatched, we found an augmenting path—return True. If matched, recursively call DFS on the left vertex that is currently matched to this right vertex (if not visited), and if that DFS succeeds, we can rematch that left vertex and free up the right vertex for the current one. Time complexity is O(m) per call in worst case as we traverse edges. Space is O(n) for recursion stack and visited array. This feeds into L0 by being called for each left vertex. Common mistakes: not handling visited array correctly (must be reset per DFS call in L0), infinite recursion if cycles exist (but bipartite graphs have no odd cycles). Real code pattern: def dfs(u): visited[u]=True; for v in adj[u]: if matchR[v]==-1 or (not visited[matchR[v]] and dfs(matchR[v])): matchR[v]=u; return True; return False.

examples.input: Left vertex: 0, matchR = [-1,-1], adj[0] = [0,1], visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: visited[0]=True. Step 2: Check neighbor v=0: matchR[0]==-1 (unmatched), so set matchR[0]=0, return True. No further neighbors checked.

examples.input: Left vertex: 1, matchR = [0,-1], adj[1] = [0], visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: visited[1]=True. Step 2: Check neighbor v=0: matchR[0]==0 (matched to left vertex 0). Recursively call dfs(0): visited[0]=True; check neighbors of 0 (e.g., adj[0]=[0,1]): v=0 is matched to 0 (self), skip; v=1: matchR[1]==-1, set matchR[1]=0, return True. Back to dfs(1): since dfs(0) returned True, set matchR[0]=1, return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Restoration / Augmenting Path Flipping
ladders.desc: The concept of finding an augmenting path and flipping matches to increase matching size.
ladders.explanation: The core insight in bipartite matching is the augmenting path: a path that starts at an unmatched left vertex, alternates between unmatched and matched edges, and ends at an unmatched right vertex. Flipping the matches along this path (i.e., changing unmatched edges to matched and vice versa) increases the total matching size by one. Intuition: each augmenting path represents a way to 'reconfigure' existing matches to include one more vertex pair without conflicts. Algorithm: 1. Identify an augmenting path via search (e.g., DFS). 2. For each edge in the path: if it was unmatched, mark it as matched; if matched, mark it as unmatched. This flipping swaps the roles, freeing up vertices. Time per flip is O(L) where L is path length, typically O(n). Space is O(1) extra. This connects to L1 as the action taken when DFS returns True. Common mistakes: flipping incorrectly (e.g., not alternating), or not handling the path endpoints properly. Real code pattern: In DFS, when an augmenting path is found, we update matchR array recursively during backtracking.

examples.input: Augmenting path: left0-right0 (unmatched), matchR initially all -1
examples.trace: Path has one edge: left0-right0. Since right0 is unmatched, flip: set matchR[0]=0. Now left0 is matched to right0.

examples.input: Augmenting path: left1-right0 (matched to left0), left0-right1 (unmatched), matchR initially [0,-1]
examples.trace: Path: left1-right0 (matched), left0-right1 (unmatched). Flip: for edge left1-right0: was matched (matchR[0]=0), so unmatch (set matchR[0]=-1 temporarily, but in practice update directly). For edge left0-right1: was unmatched, so match (set matchR[1]=0). But since we flip along path, we actually set matchR[0]=1 and matchR[1]=0. Final: left1 matched to right0, left0 matched to right1.

testCases.expected: Invalid path (not alternating), but if valid: matchR=[2,1]
Invalid path (ends at same right), but if forced: matchR=[1,-1]
Invalid path (same left), but if forced: matchR=[0,0]

ladders.title: Building Block — Bipartite Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Representing the bipartite graph and tracking matches between left and right vertices.
ladders.explanation: To solve bipartite matching, we need an efficient representation of the graph and a way to track current matches. The bipartite graph is typically represented using adjacency lists: for each left vertex, store a list of connected right vertices. This allows O(degree) neighbor access. The matching is tracked with an array matchR where matchR[v] = u if right vertex v is matched to left vertex u, else -1. Intuition: this array lets us quickly check if a right vertex is matched and to which left vertex, which is crucial during DFS. Steps: 1. Build adjacency list from edge list. 2. Initialize matchR with -1. Time to build is O(n + m), space is O(n + m). This connects to L2 by providing the data structures needed for augmenting path search. Common mistakes: using incorrect indices (0-based vs 1-based), not handling disconnected vertices, or using dense matrices for large graphs (inefficient). Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(num_left)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); matchR = [-1]*num_right.

examples.input: Left vertices: 2, Right vertices: 2, Edges: [(0,0), (0,1), (1,0)]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj as list of lists for 2 left vertices: adj = [[], []]. Step 2: Add edge (0,0): adj[0].append(0) -> adj = [[0], []]. Step 3: Add edge (0,1): adj[0].append(1) -> adj = [[0,1], []]. Step 4: Add edge (1,0): adj[1].append(0) -> adj = [[0,1], [0]]. Step 5: matchR = [-1,-1] for 2 right vertices.

examples.input: Left vertices: 1, Right vertices: 3, Edges: [(0,0), (0,2)]
examples.trace: Step 1: adj = [[]] for 1 left vertex. Step 2: Add edges: adj[0].append(0) -> [[0]]; adj[0].append(2) -> [[0,2]]. Step 3: matchR = [-1,-1,-1] for 3 right vertices.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Matching Dictionary Updates
ladders.desc: Performing depth-first search to explore vertices and edges in a graph.
ladders.explanation: Depth-first search (DFS) is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. Intuition: DFS uses a stack (implicit via recursion or explicit) to visit vertices, ensuring we fully explore one path before moving to others. Steps: 1. Start at a source vertex, mark it visited. 2. For each neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS. 3. Continue until all reachable vertices are visited. Time complexity is O(V + E) for adjacency list, space is O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. This connects to L3 as the traversal method used on the bipartite graph representation. Common mistakes: not marking visited before recursion (causing cycles), or using DFS on directed graphs without considering direction. Real code pattern: def dfs(u): visited[u]=True; for v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs(v).

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2; edges: (0,1), (1,2), start at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited[0]=True. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: [1]. Since 1 not visited, call dfs(1). Step 3: In dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True; neighbors: [2]. Since 2 not visited, call dfs(2). Step 4: In dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True; neighbors: [] (none). Return to dfs(1), then dfs(0). Done.

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2; edges: (0,1), (0,2), start at 0
examples.output: Visited order: 0,1,2 (or 0,2,1 depending on neighbor order)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, visited[0]=True. Step 2: Neighbors: [1,2]. Suppose we take 1 first: call dfs(1). Step 3: dfs(1): visited[1]=True; neighbors: [] (none). Return to 0. Step 4: Next neighbor of 0: 2, call dfs(2). Step 5: dfs(2): visited[2]=True; neighbors: [] (none). Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Bipartite Matching Basics
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of bipartite graphs and maximum matching.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph has two sets of vertices with edges only between sets. Maximum matching pairs as many left vertices to right vertices as possible without sharing vertices. DFS finds augmenting paths to increase matching size iteratively.

examples.trace: Step 1: Divide vertices: left set {0,1}, right set {2,3}. Step 2: Check edges: (0,2) connects left to right, (1,3) connects left to right. No edges within sets. So bipartite.

Step 1: Try to divide into two sets. Suppose set A={0}, set B={1}. Edge (0,1) okay. Add vertex 2: if in set A, edge (2,0) within set A—not allowed; if in set B, edge (1,2) within set B—not allowed. Cycle of length 3 (odd) exists, so not bipartite.
